shop_address: Cannaregio, 30100 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text:
Surprising oasis of peace in a hectic city.  Come here, sit, walk your dog and enjoy the quiet.
Parco di Villa Groggia is a serene, lesser-known park in Venice's Cannaregio district, offering a peaceful retreat from the city's bustle. Visitors can enjoy shaded paths, benches, and the charming Teatrino Groggia, a small 19th-century theater hosting community events .

Historically, the park occupies the site of the 16th-century Palazzo Donà, demolished in 1823. In the late 19th century, Giuseppe Groggia developed the area into a romantic-style garden. After his death, the property was bequeathed to the San Gioacchino Institute in Mestre for the education of underprivileged girls. Since 1975, the City of Venice has owned and maintained the park .

Today, Parco di Villa Groggia serves as a tranquil space for locals and visitors alike, featuring shaded areas, benches, and a community center, making it an ideal spot to relax and experience a quieter side of Venice.

shop_address: Calle Giazzo, 30122 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text: The bienalle for architecture is both mind blowing and overwhelming. The 2025 installation was very focused on climate change, global growth and usage of resources and how materials/technologies are used for global problem solving. It is important to go in with an open mind and time to process the information. Some countries and exhibits are better than others in terms of interpretation and information. For the year 2025, the beauty is in the details. The exhibition u Is not about architecture as many think of it, but rather about elements of architecture. And, if you are not comfortable with the English or Italian language, you will likely be lost and confused. It is wise to get the three-day pass and break up your time to allow the ability to process the density of information. There are many restaurants and cafes in the Giardini but in the main bienalle exhibit in the arsenale area there is only one large cafe and restaurant. Be sure to download area maps before you explore to get a good sense of place and understand the exhibits before you explore. The water taxis will bring you to the main stops for arsenale and giardini.

review_text: Beautiful location for the biennial art and architecture Biennales. At the Giardini are most of the national pavilions. At the 2025 Architecture Biennale I was particularly impressed by the US and Scandinavian pavilions with the former focusing on the porch in US architecture with great examples like Ocean Grove, New Jersey and Chautauqua, New York. The Greek pavilion was impressive as well with a focus on the two Venetian shipyards on the island of Crete. The Israeli pavilion was closed for renovation as was the pavilion of Venezuela, designed by Carlo Scarpa (1906-1978) who also designed the original entrance pavilion to the Biennale.

review_text:
Amid the bustling canals and throngs of visitors that define Venice, the Giardini della Biennale offers a breath of calm. This leafy enclave, tucked along the Bacino di San Marco, isn’t a destination for grand botanical wonders, but its quiet charm and historical roots make it a worthy detour for those seeking respite.

Originally marshland transformed by Napoleon into public gardens, the space now serves dual purposes: a peaceful retreat and a cultural hub. Its winding paths lead visitors past 30 pavilions, each representing a different nation and showcasing architectural flair from icons like Carlo Scarpa and Alvar Aalto. Though the structures truly come alive during the Venice Biennale Art Festival—when global creativity takes center stage—they retain a quiet magnetism year-round.

The gardens’ unpretentious beauty is amplified by quirky details: feral cats lounging in sunlit corners, the stoic gaze of Garibaldi’s statue at the entrance, and a serene coastal walk that skirts the water’s edge. These elements stitch together a patchwork of tranquility, a stark contrast to the nearby chaos of St. Mark’s Square.

Yet, for all its understated appeal, the Giardini della Biennale remains a “nice-to-have” rather than a must-see. If your Venice itinerary is packed, you won’t miss a life-changing experience by skipping it. But for those with moments to spare, it’s a gentle reminder that even in a city overrun by tourism, pockets of stillness—and a few curious cats—still endure.

shop_address: Viale Trento, 30122 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text: The gardens were created in 1807 by order of Napoleon Bonaparte and designed by Gianantonio Selva as a public green space for the citizens of Venice. Today, they serve both as a peaceful retreat and as a major cultural venue. Since 1895, the gardens have hosted the Venice Biennale, beginning with its first International Art Exhibition. During the Biennale, approximately 65% of the gardens are dedicated to exhibition space, while the remaining 35% remains open as public parkland. Starting in 1907, individual nations began constructing their own pavilions within the gardens. Today, there are 29 national pavilions, making the gardens one of the central hubs for contemporary art during the Biennale.

review_text:
“Tiny green escape in the heart of Venice—surprisingly refreshing! 🌿”

Stumbled upon Giardini Reali just behind Piazza San Marco, and it instantly became my go-to oasis in the city. With shaded benches, pergola-covered walkways, and a lovely view of the lagoon, it’s the perfect spot to recharge between sightseeing bursts  .

Unexpected calm: Even on busy days, the garden’s tucked-away location makes it feel almost secret—perfect for a pause with a book or a snack  .

Lovely design touches: A restored 19th-century greenhouse, drawbridge to Piazza San Marco, and the longest wisteria pergola in Venice give it both charm and elegance  .

Free and easy access: Open to the public, and ideal for hanging out while you wait for the vaporetto at Giardinetti stop. No tickets, no rush  .

review_text:
Tucked away just around the corner from the bustling St. Mark’s Square, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation (Giardini della Fondazione Venezia) are a true hidden gem—a quiet haven of greenery and tranquility in a city better known for its canals and narrow alleys. We stumbled upon this secret spot during our visit and ended up spending a wonderfully peaceful hour here, simply relaxing with an ice cream and enjoying the natural beauty.

The gardens have an interesting history, originally part of the private grounds of a noble Venetian palace. Today, they are one of the few green spaces in the historic center, offering a rare escape from the crowds. The well-maintained paths wind through a delightful mix of Mediterranean plants, shady trees, and blooming flowers, creating an atmosphere that feels worlds away from the nearby tourist hotspots.

One of the most enjoyable aspects was the sense of seclusion—even though we were only minutes from the heart of Venice, the gardens felt almost undiscovered. The benches and quiet corners make it an ideal spot to pause, unwind, and soak in the beauty of the surroundings.

For anyone looking for a moment of calm in the midst of Venice’s energy, this is the perfect place. Whether you’re escaping the midday heat, looking for a quiet place to read, or simply wanting to appreciate another side of the city, the Gardens of the Venice Foundation are well worth seeking out.

review_text:
The park in Italy was simple but beautiful, with wide stretches of bright green grass that looked almost painted under the sunlight. It was the kind of place where everything felt calm and open. The grass was soft and freshly cut, carrying that clean, earthy smell that comes after a morning watering. People sat on blankets or benches, talking quietly or just watching the day go by.
Children ran across the open field, their shoes kicking up tiny bits of grass as they played, and every so often a light breeze would move through, making the leaves in the nearby trees rustle softly. There were a few old stone paths that curved through the park, leading to shaded spots where the grass grew a little taller and wilder.
From one end, you could see the red roofs of nearby houses and hear the faint sounds of a café — clinking cups, laughter, and the hiss of an espresso machine. It wasn’t a large park, but it had a peaceful feeling, like it belonged to the people who used it every day. The green grass stretched out like a soft carpet, inviting anyone to sit, rest, or just enjoy the view. It was simple, sunny, and perfectly Italian in its quiet charm

review_text:
This was a beautiful experience. There is a small tour which gives you enough information to inform and make the experience meaningful, but the tour guide allows you to experience the walk and music with yourself. The music composed specifically for the walk through the labyrinth is gorgeous, and walking quietly through while listening is a meditative experience, even if you are not a Borges readers. It was lightly raining, with sun, while we were there which added to the beauty!

We did this as a paired tour with the Vatican Chapels, and together they were an excellent experience.

We would love to experience it in another season, and will probably go back! Thank you!

Know before you go:
-You must book a ticket in advance through the Cini Foundation website.
-This is a symbolic labyrinth, not meant to be like a tourist corn maze or the like, more akin to a meditation garden.
-You should bring your own headphones if you can.

shop_address: Via Orlanda, 30173 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text: Beautiful area with lots of green space, a park in the heart of Mestre and just across from Venice. The perfect place for complete rest and relaxation. Inside the park are great restaurants and bars that offer good food and drink. The green area offers a good view of Venice and the Venetian lagoon. Very open, with lots of trails for both runners and walkers and cyclists. Everything is great done. It is easy to walk or run 4-5 km around the park, the lagoon and its trails. Apart from being an area for environmental and landscape recovery, it is also one of the most important places to study the lagoon environment, it is also popular as a destination for sports and cultural activities for leisure. An ideal place to stay in nature with the family. Created thanks to the European contribution, San Giuliano Park is an ambitious project, one of the most significant urban conversion and land reclamation interventions in Italy due to its complexity and articulation, as well as its dimensional and territorial characteristics. San Giuliano Park is one of the richest areas of biodiversity in the Venetian lagoon environment, and is known for being an excellent area for bird watching to visit the lagoon, attracting the interest of many nature photographers and observers, especially ornithologists. The park is actually known to enthusiasts for its rich avifauna, as it is a nesting area for many species of birds.

review_text: Fantastic service. I actually ended up getting stuck in shallower waters near the Marina, and the Mariner came to assist. Once I was on shore, a very kind young gentleman in his early 20's brought me some first aid supplies. Very memorable in a good way.

shop_address: Isola della Certosa, 30141 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text: This is a fantastic Marina with Venice at your doorstep. We have an annual berth. The process went smoothly from our visit while we were on holidays in Venice. All the staff have spent some time with us showing us the Marina and the surroundings. We even could see our berth before signing the contract. Upon arrival this morning at 7.15 am from Croatia, the Harbour Master was awaiting for us to guide us to our berth and helped us secure the boat. The location is amazing if you love Venice and nature altogether as this island, which is a nature park and quite large, is a vaporetto stop away from the city, but is very quiet! The bathrooms are just of another level compared to any other marinas. They are like the Italian bathrooms you have at home and they are spotless. You have toilettes and shower in the same large private room. 5 star service! Amazing! Well done to the entire staff!

shop_address: Isola della Certosa, 30141 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text: What a wonderful marina! The marina is located on a beautiful island next to Venice city with a lovely nature and park-garden. You can reach Venice city by easily by water bus and in the evening the marina staff offers water taxis for free. The showers and toilets are brand new and in a very good condition.

review_text: Il parco Emmer, come avrebbero voluto da sempre abitanti e frequentatori, sta lentamente diventando una delle aree verdi più apprezzate del quartiere. Il parco si trova in Via P.Cornaglia a poca distanza dal Centro Commerciale Panorama. Il bellissimo parco offre un posto meraviglioso per rilassarsi, fare jogging, fare una piacevole passeggiata, divertirsi con i bambini o giocare a calcetto. Il parco prende il nome dal famoso architetto Pietro Emilio Emmer che costruì gran parte di Marghera nel 1919. Ha prestato particolare attenzione alle aree verdi e ai parchi.

Got looked up and down by a couple of Africans on the way in and instantly saw multiple groups of young men some wearing no shirts and drinking, all checking us out awkwardly. This place looked more like a prison yard than a public green space.

Quickly left to avoid conflict or being robbed.

shop_address: Via Circonvallazione, 30171 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text: This is a small but very refined park in the center of Mestre. Villa Querini has a formal garden at the front and a more informal, romantically planted area at the back. The park has benches, a children's playground, a herb garden, an enclosed dog park and bike lanes. The park isn't too big so it's easier to keep track of your kids. The Marzenego River flows along the park and it just makes the whole place shine! This park was one of the only ones in the city before the city administrations decided to plant more trees and parks. I certainly suggest you visit the place if happen to be in Mestre.

Letteralmente piatto, la collina è stata buttata giù  e ora sono stati piantati degli alberelli, ovviamente messi in riga.

shop_address: 30100 Venezia VE, Italia
review_text:
Parco Albanese di Bissuola interessa un’area di 33 ettari di cui 21 ettari di impianti prativi restante 12 ettari di aree a bosco e superfici pavimentate (piazzali, vialetti, superfici da gioco ecc.) e superfici acquee.

Le aree del Parco Albanese di Bissuola comprendono: aree verdi a prato ed arbustive, aree laghetto e oasi ornitologica, giochi d’acqua, percorsi pedonali e ciclabili, impianti sportivi e ricreativi (campetti da calcio, pallavolo, basket, tennis, bocce, pattinaggio, piscina, bar e ristorante, area giochi attrezzata) parcheggi per auto e motocicli. All’interno del Parco si trovano anche il Teatro del Parco, la Biblioteca di quartiere, l’Orto Botanico Locatelli, annesso agli orti comunali. Nelle Zone prative ed arbustive, le specie botaniche più rappresentate sono quelle tipiche dei boschi della pianura padano/veneta, quali Populus nigra, Carpinus betulus, Qercus pedunculata; fraxinus oxicarpa, Acer pseudoplatanus, Tilia cordata, e Tilia platyphillos, Salix alba, Alnus glutinosa ed alcune specie “esotiche” Salisburia adiantifolia (Gingko biloba), Celtis australis, Abies cephalonica e Cedrus deodara; In totale gli alberi sono 1.370. Circa 1300 le piante di rose, rose Multiflora e Mutabilis che fiancheggiano il lato NO dello stagno palmipedi e rampicanti che coprono il pergolato dell’isola rialzata in mattoni facciavista .
